questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the four components of the patient history?
What is the purpose of the Family Medical History?

answers.text: Learn the health status of blood relatives and screen for hereditary-linked diseases

questions.text: Which history category gathers information about possible asbestos, coal dust, or fume exposure?
Which chart document is written by the admitting physician and provides the patient's baseline status?
What is the primary purpose of a Progress Note?

answers.text: List treatments the physician believes are needed
Provide daily annotations detailing progress toward treatment goals

questions.text: For a patient with a DNR/DNI/CMO order, what three steps must be taken?

answers.text: Verify the order in the chart, ensure the wristband is in place, and discuss with the care team
Cancel all medications, remove IV access, and discharge the patient
Notify the family and proceed with intubation

questions.text: What are the four main categories of laboratory values covered in this lesson?

answers.text: BMP, CMP, Gram stain, Pulse oximetry

questions.text: What is the normal range for total white blood cell count?
Which WBC type is elevated with allergic reactions and is associated with asthma?
Elevation of which WBC type is associated with tuberculosis?
What differentiates band neutrophils from segmented neutrophils?

answers.text: Bands are mature cells; segmented are immature
Bands are immature cells that increase with bacterial infection; segmented are mature cells that decrease with bacterial infection
They are identical — just different names
Bands come from lymph nodes; segmented come from bone marrow

questions.text: A WBC count of 18,000 would indicate what condition, and what intervention does it suggest?

answers.text: Leukocytosis — indication for the patient to be on antibiotics

questions.text: Leukopenia is a decrease in WBC that places the patient at greatest risk for what?
What is the normal range for red blood cell count?
Why does polycythemia develop in a patient with chronic COPD or someone living at high altitude?

answers.text: The kidneys excrete extra RBCs to raise hematocrit
The body releases erythropoietin (EPO) in response to low tissue oxygen, stimulating bone marrow to make more RBCs

questions.text: A major complication of polycythemia is:
How much oxygen does 1 gram of hemoglobin carry?
What is the normal hemoglobin range for an adult male?
Hematocrit primarily reflects which aspect of the patient's status?
A platelet count of 120,000 indicates which condition?

answers.text: Thrombocytosis — the patient is at risk of clotting
Thrombocytopenia — the patient is likely to have bleeding problems

questions.text: Sodium's normal range and role:
Which electrolyte is the primary intracellular cation, critical for heart and kidney function and acid-base balance?
Hyperkalemia can be caused by which of the following?
Chloride's normal range and primary role is:
An increased Total CO₂ (TCO₂) is most commonly caused by:
Normal fasting glucose range is:
BUN and Creatinine are primarily used to assess which organ system?
An elevated BUN suggests _____; a decreased BUN suggests _____.

answers.text: Kidney disease; liver disease or malnutrition

questions.text: The purpose of a Sputum Gram Stain is:

answers.text: To measure the patient's hemoglobin level
To determine the specific microorganism causing an infection and guide antibiotic choice
To assess the patient's coagulation status
To measure the patient's oxygen level

questions.text: Which organisms are typically identified by a Gram POSITIVE sputum result?

answers.text: Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Haemophilus influenzae, E. coli

questions.text: Gram NEGATIVE sputum results (Pseudomonas, H. influenzae, E. coli) are typically treated with:
Which test is a rapid and effective method for determining tuberculosis?
How long does it typically take to get sputum culture results back?
Prothrombin Time (PT) is specifically indicated to monitor patients on which anticoagulant?
Normal PT range is:
An INR that is too high means:

answers.text: The patient's blood clots very fast
The patient may be in danger of bleeding out

questions.text: APTT (normal 23 – 32 seconds) is indicated to monitor patients on which drug?
